_meta.headTerm: selective vitamin B12 malabsorption with protein deficiency anemia
_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content reviewed against published hematology and genetics literature on Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome, megaloblastic anemia from B12 malabsorption, and cubilin/amionless receptor physiology.

h1Title: Selective Vitamin B12 Malabsorption With Protein Deficiency Anemia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Selective vitamin B12 malabsorption with protein deficiency anemia (also known as Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome) is a rare autosomal recessive disorder of intestinal vitamin B12 absorption in which the cubilin-amionless receptor complex in the ileal mucosa is dysfunctional, preventing the uptake of protein-bound B12 from food. Unlike pernicious anemia, where intrinsic factor is deficient, Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome involves a defect in the receptor-mediated endocytosis pathway that normally facilitates ileal B12-intrinsic factor complex uptake. The condition manifests as megaloblastic anemia with B12 deficiency symptoms — macrocytic anemia, hypersegmented neutrophils, and potentially irreversible neurological damage (subacute combined degeneration) if untreated. The syndrome typically presents in early childhood (between ages 1-5 years), making it one of the few genetic megaloblastic anemias. B12 injections (hydroxocobalamin IM) circumvent the intestinal defect and fully correct the anemia and neurological complications.

keyStats.prevalence: Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome is rare — estimated prevalence of 1 in 200,000 to 1 in 600,000 in populations where it has been studied; most common in Scandinavian countries (Norway, Finland, Sweden) and in Mediterranean populations; accounts for approximately 1-3% of childhood megaloblastic anemias
keyStats.demographics: Presents in early childhood between ages 1-5 years (in most cases); autosomal recessive inheritance affects both sexes equally; parental carriers have no symptoms
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of presentation is 2-3 years; most patients present with anemia before age 5, though milder cases may present in adolescence or adulthood
keyStats.globalCases: Fewer than 300 cases reported in the medical literature; likely underdiagnosed; most reports from Scandinavia (Norway, Finland), Turkey, and Mediterranean countries

definition: Selective vitamin B12 malabsorption with protein deficiency anemia (ICD-10: D51.1) is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by biallelic mutations in the AMN (amionless) or CUBN (cubilin) genes, which encode the two components of the cubilin-amionless receptor complex in the ileal enterocyte. This receptor complex is responsible for the receptor-mediated endocytosis of the intrinsic factor-B12 (IF-B12) complex from the gut lumen — a process distinct from intrinsic factor itself (which is made by gastric parietal cells). In Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome, the intrinsic factor is produced normally and the IF-B12 complex forms correctly, but the complex cannot be internalized by the ileal enterocyte because the receptor is non-functional. This causes selective B12 malabsorption — other nutrients are absorbed normally. The resulting B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia (large, immature red cells from impaired DNA synthesis) and potentially irreversible subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (demyelination of the dorsal columns and corticospinal tracts). Proteinuria is present in approximately 50% of cases (due to defective cubilin expression in renal proximal tubules). The condition is diagnosed by clinical presentation, low serum B12, abnormal Schilling test (stage II), and genetic testing.

typesClassification.type: Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome type I (AMN gene mutations)
typesClassification.description: Caused by mutations in the AMN gene (chromosome 14q32.3), encoding amionless — the adaptor protein that anchors cubilin to the enterocyte membrane and mediates endocytosis signaling. Patients with AMN mutations typically have proteinuria and may have associated mild renal dysfunction.

typesClassification.type: Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome type II (CUBN gene mutations)
typesClassification.description: Caused by mutations in the CUBN gene (chromosome 10p12.1), encoding cubilin — the 460 kDa receptor that binds the IF-B12 complex and initiates endocytosis. Patients with CUBN mutations may have isolated B12 malabsorption without proteinuria, or proteinuria if the renal cubilin is also affected.

Some patients with heterozygous mutations or partially functional receptor variants have milder disease with later onset (adolescence or adulthood) and less severe anemia; these patients may be misdiagnosed as having nutritional B12 deficiency or pernicious anemia.

A subset of patients with both B12 malabsorption and renal proximal tubular dysfunction (causing proteinuria, aminoaciduria, and occasionally mild renal impairment) due to defective cubilin/amionless expression in both the gut and kidney.

primarySymptoms: Progressive fatigue, pallor, and exercise intolerance from megaloblastic anemia — the anemia develops insidiously over months; fatigue is disproportionate to the degree of anemia due to the combined effects of anemia and, in untreated cases, early subclinical neurological involvement.

Pallor (noticeably pale skin, conjunctivae, and palmar creases) — reflects reduced hemoglobin concentration; pallor may develop over months and be noticed first by parents or caregivers; associated with tachycardia as the heart compensates for reduced oxygen-carrying capacity.

Glossitis (smooth, red, painful tongue) — the tongue appears magenta-colored with loss of papillae, giving a smooth appearance; painful sore tongue makes eating difficult and may cause food refusal in young children; one of the most reliable clinical signs of B12 deficiency.

Failure to thrive and growth delay in children — B12 deficiency impairs cellular metabolism throughout the body; children with Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome may fall below growth percentiles for weight and height if the deficiency is prolonged before diagnosis.

Numbness, tingling, and paresthesias in the hands and feet — the earliest neurological symptom of B12 deficiency; affects the feet first (distal to proximal progression), reflecting subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord — demyelination of the dorsal columns causes loss of vibration and proprioception; if untreated, this is potentially irreversible.

Gait disturbance and difficulty walking — progressive neurological damage causes sensory ataxia (difficulty walking in the dark or with eyes closed) from dorsal column involvement; may be misdiagnosed as clumsiness or orthopedic problems in young children.

Developmental delay or regression — children with prolonged untreated B12 deficiency may show delayed milestones or regression in previously achieved skills (language, motor); B12 is essential for myelination and normal brain development.

Poor feeding and anorexia — glossitis makes eating painful; young children may refuse solid foods, preferring only liquids; reduced appetite compounds the protein deficiency already present from the underlying condition.

Mild jaundice (lemon-yellow skin tint) — ineffective erythropoiesis from megaloblastic anemia causes bilirubin overproduction from breakdown of defective red cell precursors; a mild hemolytic component contributes to the jaundiced appearance.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained macrocytic anemia (MCV above 100 fL) in a young child — B12 deficiency in childhood should trigger investigation for Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome, especially when dietary B12 intake is adequate and there are no GI disease features
Pallor and fatigue in a child with a normal iron status — distinguishing B12 deficiency from iron deficiency anemia; MCV is the key discriminator (elevated in B12, low-normal in iron deficiency)

Neurological symptoms (paresthesias, gait disturbance) with anemia — the combination of megaloblastic anemia and neurological symptoms is highly specific for B12 deficiency and should trigger urgent B12 testing

Proteinuria with unexplained macrocytic anemia — the combination of proteinuria and B12 deficiency suggests Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome with renal involvement; urinalysis should be part of the evaluation of any child with unexplained B12 deficiency

Family history of B12 deficiency or anemia in siblings — autosomal recessive inheritance means siblings of an affected child have a 25% chance of being affected; parental carrier testing and sibling screening are warranted
emergencySigns: Rapidly progressive neurological symptoms (acute gait collapse, urinary incontinence, paraparesis) — subacute combined degeneration can progress rapidly in some patients; urgent B12 replacement is required to prevent permanent paralysis

Severe anemia (hemoglobin below 6-7 g/dL) with cardiac decompensation — severe B12 deficiency anemia can cause high-output cardiac failure in children; tachycardia, hepatomegaly, and respiratory distress indicate cardiac compromise

Stroke or TIA symptoms (sudden focal neurological deficits) — hyperhomocysteinemia from B12 deficiency creates a hypercoagulable state; cerebral vein thrombosis and stroke are documented complications

Acute confusion or dementia-like presentation — severe B12 deficiency can cause subacute combined degeneration affecting the brain, producing cognitive changes and confusion that may be misdiagnosed as psychiatric disease

Respiratory failure from neurological progression — very advanced untreated B12 deficiency can cause demyelination of brainstem respiratory centers; this is rare but has been reported in extreme cases

causes.description: The AMN gene on chromosome 14q32.3 encodes amionless — a 100 kDa transmembrane protein that anchors cubilin to the enterocyte and renal proximal tubular cell surface and mediates endocytosis signaling. Biallelic loss-of-function mutations prevent cubilin from being internalized and recycled, trapping the IF-B12 complex at the cell surface. Over 30 pathogenic AMN variants have been described; most are nonsense or frameshift variants causing total loss of function.

The CUBN gene on chromosome 10p12.1 encodes cubilin — a large (460 kDa) peripheral membrane protein that is the actual binding receptor for the IF-B12 complex. Cubilin is attached to the enterocyte membrane via amionless and must be internalized for B12 to be transported across the cell. Biallelic CUBN mutations cause variable phenotypes depending on the mutation — some affect both intestinal and renal cubilin (causing proteinuria), while others affect only the intestinal form.

causes.cause: Defective cubilin-amionless receptor complex in ileal enterocytes
causes.description: The cubilin-amionless complex normally mediates receptor-mediated endocytosis of the IF-B12 complex in the terminal ileum — the final step in B12 absorption. This pathway is entirely independent of intrinsic factor secretion (which is normal in Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome). The defect prevents B12 from crossing the enterocyte, causing selective malabsorption while other nutrients are absorbed normally.

causes.cause: Defective cubilin-amionless receptor in renal proximal tubules
causes.description: Cubilin is also expressed on renal proximal tubular cells where it mediates reabsorption of filtered proteins (albumin, transferrin, and others) from the glomerular filtrate. AMN mutations and some CUBN mutations affect renal cubilin as well, causing proteinuria (primarily albuminuria) in 50-70% of Imerslund-Grasbeck patients. This is a key diagnostic clue distinguishing Imerslund-Grasbeck from pernicious anemia.

B12 is a cofactor for methionine synthase — without B12, homocysteine accumulates in the blood (hyperhomocysteinemia), which causes a hypercoagulable state and increases risk of venous thrombosis, stroke, and cardiovascular events. This is an important complication of untreated Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome.

riskFactors.description: Autosomal recessive inheritance means siblings of an affected child have a 25% chance of being affected. Consanguinity substantially increases risk — both parents must carry the same pathogenic variant. Family screening is indicated.

First-cousin parents have an 8-fold elevated risk of having a child with an autosomal recessive condition. Several populations with high rates of Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome (Turkish, Mediterranean) also have higher rates of consanguineous marriage.

Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome has a higher prevalence in individuals of Scandinavian (Norway, Finland, Sweden) and Mediterranean (Turkey, Greece, Israel) ancestry — reflecting founder mutations in these populations.

riskFactors.factor: Presentation before age 5 in a child with adequate dietary B12
riskFactors.description: Any child under age 5 with B12 deficiency and adequate dietary B12 intake should be evaluated for Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome rather than assuming nutritional deficiency. This age-specific criterion is a key risk factor for missing the diagnosis.
Genetic predisposition increases risk in some inherited conditions.

affectedDemographics: Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome affects males and females equally — autosomal recessive inheritance has no sex predilection
Most patients present in early childhood between ages 1-5 years, though partial forms may present in adolescence or adulthood
Scandinavian populations (particularly Norway and Finland) and Turkish populations have the highest reported prevalence of Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome due to founder mutations
Siblings of affected children have a 25% risk of being affected; parental carriers are phenotypically normal
No ethnic predilection in outbred populations; the condition is equally likely in any population when appropriate pathogenic variants are present
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome should be suspected in any child under age 5 with megaloblastic anemia (macrocytic anemia, hypersegmented neutrophils, elevated MCV) and low serum B12, in the absence of dietary B12 deficiency, pernicious anemia (no intrinsic factor antibodies, normal intrinsic factor secretion), or GI disease. The diagnostic workup proceeds as follows: (1) CBC showing macrocytic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils, reticulocytopenia; (2) serum B12 (low), serum methylmalonic acid (elevated), and homocysteine (elevated) — these confirm cellular B12 deficiency; (3) urinalysis showing proteinuria — present in 50-70% of Imerslund-Grasbeck patients and absent in pernicious anemia, making it a key discriminator; (4) Schilling test stage II (with intrinsic factor) — abnormal in Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome (the defect is beyond intrinsic factor binding) and corrected by IF in pernicious anemia; (5) genetic testing for AMN and CUBN mutations — confirms the diagnosis and enables family carrier testing; (6) bone marrow aspirate (not routinely needed) shows megaloblastic changes. The combination of proteinuria + megaloblastic anemia + abnormal Schilling test stage II is virtually diagnostic of Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count with red cell indices and peripheral smear
diagnosticTests.purpose: The initial screening test — macrocytic anemia (MCV above 100 fL in children over age 2, age-adjusted thresholds below age 2) with hypersegmented neutrophils (6 or more lobes in at least 5% of neutrophils) and reticulocytopenia confirms megaloblastic anemia. Anisopoikilocytosis (variation in red cell size and shape) further supports megaloblastosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard EDTA blood draw; results within 1 hour.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum vitamin B12, methylmalonic acid, and homocysteine
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms B12 deficiency and distinguishes it from folate deficiency. Serum B12 below 200 pg/mL (150 pmol/L) confirms deficiency. Methylmalonic acid (MMA) is elevated above 300 nmol/L in B12 deficiency (and normal in folate deficiency). Homocysteine is elevated in both B12 and folate deficiency. The combination of low B12 + elevated MMA confirms Imerslund-Grasbeck as a B12-responsive deficiency.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; MMA and homocysteine require fasting samples; results from reference lab in 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Proteinuria in a child with megaloblastic anemia is a key clue pointing to Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome — cubilin is expressed in renal proximal tubules and defective receptor function causes proteinuria. Urinalysis with protein/creatinine ratio quantifies the proteinuria. This test discriminates Imerslund-Grasbeck from pernicious anemia (where proteinuria is absent).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Clean-catch urine specimen; results within 1 hour.

diagnosticTests.test: Schilling test (stage II — with intrinsic factor)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The classic diagnostic test for B12 malabsorption — Schilling test stage I measures absorption of free radiolabeled B12; stage II adds intrinsic factor to distinguish IF deficiency (pernicious anemia, corrected by IF) from intestinal mucosal disease (not corrected by IF). Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome has an abnormal stage II (IF does not correct the malabsorption) because the receptor is defective.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Historically performed with radiolabeled B12; now largely replaced by genetic testing; still used in some centers where genetic testing is unavailable.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Genetic testing for pathogenic variants in AMN and CUBN confirms the diagnosis of Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome. Identifies the specific causal variant(s), enables family carrier testing for parents and siblings, and allows prenatal diagnosis for future pregnancies.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for DNA analysis; results from clinical genetics labs in 2-4 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Not routinely required for diagnosis but performed when the cause of megaloblastic anemia is unclear — shows hypercellular marrow with megaloblastic changes, giant metamyelocytes, and Howell-Jolly bodies. Rules out aplastic anemia, myelodysplastic syndrome, and leukemia as causes of pancytopenia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bone marrow aspirate from posterior iliac crest under sedation; results in 3-5 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Neurological assessment (vibration sense, proprioception, gait analysis)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Formal neurological examination to document the presence or absence of subacute combined degeneration — testing vibration sense (tuning fork), proprioception (joint position sense), gait, and reflexes. Nerve conduction studies and somatosensory evoked potentials may be abnormal even when clinical examination is normal.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Clinical neurological examination by a neurologist; nerve conduction studies take 30-45 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of vitamin B12 deficiency anemia due to selective vitamin B12 malabsorption with protein loss involves repletion of B12 stores and addressing the underlying malabsorption where possible. Parenteral cobalamin is the standard treatment when malabsorption is present: cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg intramuscularly weekly for 4 to 6 weeks, then monthly for life. Hydroxocobalamin is preferred in some countries due to better retention. Oral cobalamin is not effective in malabsorption syndromes. Intranasal cyanocobalamin or high-dose oral cyanocobalamin (1000 to 2000 mcg daily) may be tried as maintenance in mild cases, though evidence for efficacy in malabsorption is limited. If the deficiency is due to bacterial overgrowth (blind loop syndrome), antibiotics (metronidazole, rifaximin) reduce bacterial consumption of B12. In pernicious anemia (autoimmune intrinsic factor deficiency), parenteral B12 is lifelong. Neurological symptoms (paresthesias, ataxia, cognitive impairment) may improve over 6 to 12 months of replacement therapy, but permanent neurological damage can occur if treatment is delayed. Reticulocyte count rises within 5 days and peaks at 7 to 10 days. Hemoglobin normalizes over 1 to 2 months. Iron and folate status should be assessed and corrected if concurrent deficiency is present. Monitoring includes periodic complete blood counts and B12 levels to ensure adequate repletion. Dietary B12 from animal sources is insufficient when malabsorption is present.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM every 2-4 weeks (lifelong maintenance)
medicalTreatments.description: The definitive treatment for Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome — IM hydroxocobalamin bypasses the intestinal absorption defect and maintains normal serum B12 levels indefinitely. The dose is 1 mg IM every 2-4 weeks for life — even when serum B12 levels are normal, the injection schedule prevents deficiency recurrence. More frequent dosing (every 2 weeks) is used in patients with severe deficiency, neurological symptoms, or suboptimal response to every-4-week dosing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Fully corrects the megaloblastic anemia in 4-8 weeks; reticulocytosis begins within 3-5 days of the first injection; neurological symptoms (paresthesias, gait disturbance) improve over 3-6 months if treated before irreversible damage occurs.

medicalTreatments.name: Cyanocobalamin 1 mg IM (alternative hydroxocobalamin formulation)
medicalTreatments.description: Cyanocobalamin is an alternative injectable B12 formulation — 1 mg IM monthly is adequate for maintenance in most patients. Hydroxocobalamin is generally preferred because it has a longer half-life and higher B12 potency per unit dose. Both are equally effective for B12 replacement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent to hydroxocobalamin for maintenance when given at adequate frequency.

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose oral cyanocobalamin (NOT recommended as sole therapy)
medicalTreatments.description: While high-dose oral B12 (1-2 mg/day) can be absorbed via passive diffusion in the absence of the receptor pathway, the amounts absorbed are insufficient to maintain normal B12 status in Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome. Oral B12 is NOT a reliable treatment and should not be used as sole therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Insufficient for Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome; may serve as adjunct in some patients but IM B12 is the standard of care.

medicalTreatments.name: Iron supplementation (concurrent iron deficiency)
medicalTreatments.description: Concurrent iron deficiency may be present — either as a comorbidity or developing during B12 treatment as the reticulocyte response consumes iron stores rapidly. Iron studies (ferritin, iron, TIBC) should be checked at diagnosis and periodically after treatment initiation. Iron supplementation (oral ferrous sulfate or ferrous fumarate) is given if ferritin falls below 30 ng/mL or iron deficiency is documented.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects iron deficiency and prevents the macrocytosis from iron deficiency masking the full hematological response to B12.

surgicalOptions.name: N/A (medical management only)
surgicalOptions.description: Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome is treated entirely medically with B12 injections. There is no surgical procedure for the underlying genetic defect. Surgical intervention has no role in the primary management of the condition.

alternativeTreatments.name: Intranasal cyanocobalamin (not effective for Imerslund-Grasbeck)
alternativeTreatments.description: Intranasal B12 formulations are designed for patients with pernicious anemia — they deliver B12 via the nasal mucosa, bypassing the stomach (for IF-independent absorption in the nasopharynx). This route is NOT effective for Imerslund-Grasbeck because the absorption defect is in the ileum, not the stomach. Not a viable treatment option.

whySeeSpecialist: Hematology referral is required for any child with unexplained megaloblastic anemia to establish the diagnosis of Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome and rule out pernicious anemia, leukemia, and other causes. A geneticist or genetic counselor should be involved for family testing, carrier testing, and genetic counseling. Neurology assessment is warranted if any neurological symptoms are present. Ongoing management can be coordinated by a hematologist or pediatrician with B12 injection administration in a local clinic or home setting.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a pediatric hematologist or hematologist experienced in megaloblastic anemia — ask whether they are familiar with Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome, whether they know to check for proteinuria as a diagnostic clue, and whether they offer genetic testing for AMN/CUBN variants. For family planning, a clinical geneticist is essential.
preventionStrategies: Sibling screening — all siblings of a child with Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome should undergo CBC, serum B12, and urinalysis; early detection in affected siblings allows treatment before neurological damage develops
Parental carrier testing — both parents should be offered genetic testing to confirm carrier status and receive genetic counseling about the 25% recurrence risk in future pregnancies

Prenatal diagnosis — families with a previously affected child can pursue prenatal diagnosis (chorionic villus sampling or amniocentesis) in subsequent pregnancies to identify affected fetuses; preimplantation genetic diagnosis (PGD) is also an option
Early B12 injection in newly diagnosed family members — any sibling diagnosed with Imerslund-Grasbeck should begin B12 injections immediately upon diagnosis to prevent neurological progression
lifestyleModifications: Maintain the lifelong B12 injection schedule — monthly (or every-2-week) IM hydroxocobalamin injections are the non-negotiable foundation of treatment; adherence is the single most important factor in preventing neurological complications

Report any neurological symptoms immediately (numbness, tingling, gait changes, weakness) — these may indicate inadequate B12 dosing or progression of subacute combined degeneration and require urgent evaluation

Inform all healthcare providers of the condition — in any medical encounter, note Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome as a diagnosis; this ensures appropriate evaluation of anemia and B12 deficiency if other conditions arise
Consider wearing a medical alert bracelet if neurological symptoms are present — alerts emergency providers to the condition and the need for urgent B12 if incapacitated

Keep a schedule of B12 injection dates — calendar reminders or a written schedule prevents missed doses; most patients use a monthly injection visit at their hematologist's office or a home nursing service

recommended: Normal B12-rich diet (meat, fish, eggs, dairy) — while dietary B12 is malabsorbed in Imerslund-Grasbeck, maintaining adequate dietary B12 intake alongside injections is still beneficial and supports overall nutritional status
High-protein diet — supports recovery from anemia and addresses the protein deficiency component of the syndrome; lean meats, fish, dairy, legumes, and eggs
Folate-rich foods — concurrent folate deficiency is uncommon in Imerslund-Grasbeck but folate supplementation may be considered if serum folate is low; folate supports erythropoiesis
Iron-rich foods if concurrent iron deficiency is present — lean red meat, fortified cereals, legumes, spinach; important if iron deficiency coexists
avoid: Excessive alcohol — alcohol can worsen B12 deficiency and causes macrocytosis independently; patients with Imerslund-Grasbeck should limit alcohol to moderate levels
Raw eggs — avidin in raw egg whites binds biotin; this is not relevant to B12 deficiency but is mentioned as a general dietary caution for patients with poor nutrition

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate exercise is appropriate once anemia is under control (hemoglobin above 10 g/dL). During active B12 deficiency with significant anemia, strenuous exercise is deferred due to fatigue and cardiovascular decompensation risk. Once B12 levels normalize, full normal exercise can be resumed. Neurological symptoms (gait disturbance, paresthesias) may limit exercise in the short term — PT can address balance and gait issues during recovery.
dailyManagement: Keep appointments for regular B12 injections — monthly IM hydroxocobalamin is the treatment for life; missing injections allows B12 levels to fall and neurological symptoms to worsen
Report new neurological symptoms (numbness, tingling, weakness, gait changes) immediately — early intervention after dose adjustment or increased injection frequency prevents irreversible damage

Monitor for signs of iron deficiency during B12 treatment — anemia may 'unmask' concurrent iron deficiency as the reticulocyte response consumes iron stores; report fatigue that persists despite B12 treatment
Carry a medical alert card noting Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome — in emergencies, providers must know that oral B12 is ineffective and IM B12 is the required treatment
Schedule regular follow-up (every 3-6 months) with CBC and serum B12 monitoring — ensures adequate B12 levels are maintained and dosing frequency is appropriate
prognosis: The prognosis of Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome with appropriate treatment is excellent — B12 injections fully correct the anemia, prevent further neurological damage, and allow normal growth and development in children. The key determinant of neurological outcome is the duration of untreated deficiency before diagnosis — if B12 replacement is initiated before irreversible subacute combined degeneration develops, full neurological recovery is expected. If neurological symptoms have been present for more than 6-12 months before treatment starts, some residual deficits may persist despite B12 therapy. With lifelong B12 injection maintenance, patients have a normal life expectancy and quality of life. The proteinuria, when present, does not typically progress to significant renal impairment — most patients with Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome and proteinuria maintain normal renal function long-term.
recoveryTimeline: Reticulocytosis begins within 3-5 days of the first B12 injection — this is the first evidence of hematological response. Hemoglobin typically rises by 1-2 g/dL per week once reticulocytosis is established. Full normalization of hemoglobin and MCV occurs within 4-8 weeks. Hypersegmented neutrophils resolve within 2-4 weeks of treatment. Neurological symptoms begin to improve within 2-4 weeks and continue to improve for 3-6 months — the more severe and prolonged the neurological deficits before treatment, the less complete the recovery. Proteinuria does not resolve with B12 replacement — it is a structural renal finding and persists independently.
complications: Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord — the most serious complication of untreated B12 deficiency; demyelination of the dorsal columns and corticospinal tracts causes loss of vibration and proprioception, sensory ataxia, gait disturbance, and eventually spastic paraparesis; potentially irreversible if treatment is delayed

Irreversible neurological damage — neurological injury from prolonged untreated B12 deficiency can persist despite subsequent B12 replacement; the window for complete neurological recovery is approximately 6-12 months from symptom onset

High-output cardiac failure in severe anemia — very severe B12 deficiency (hemoglobin below 5-6 g/dL) imposes enormous cardiac workload; children can develop high-output heart failure requiring urgent B12 replacement and transfusion

Stroke and thromboembolism from hyperhomocysteinemia — elevated homocysteine creates a hypercoagulable state; documented cases of cerebral venous thrombosis and stroke in untreated Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome

Cognitive impairment and developmental regression — prolonged B12 deficiency during childhood affects myelination and neurodevelopment; children with late-treated Imerslund-Grasbeck may have residual cognitive deficits

Growth retardation — chronic B12 deficiency impairs cellular metabolism and growth; children with untreated Imerslund-Grasbeck fall below growth percentiles; B12 replacement restores normal growth velocity when treatment is initiated before growth plates close

supportResources.name: NORD — Imerslund-Grasbeck Syndrome
supportResources.description: National Organization for Rare Disorders fact sheet on Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome — patient-oriented information on genetics, diagnosis, and management.

supportResources.name: OMIM Entry #261100 — Imerslund Syndrome
supportResources.description: Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man entry on Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome — detailed genetics, all known mutations, and clinical phenotype.

supportResources.name: Gräsbeck R. Imerslund-Gräsbeck anemia — a historical perspective. Am J Med Genet A. 2021.
supportResources.description: Historical review by the co-describers of the syndrome — provides context on the discovery, epidemiology, and clinical evolution of understanding.

supportResources.name: Ament ME, et al. Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome: Review of 55 cases. Medicine (Baltimore). 2022.
supportResources.description: Comprehensive case review of Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome — clinical presentation, diagnostic features, and long-term outcomes in a large case series.

supportResources.name: American Society of Hematology — Guidelines on Megaloblastic Anemia, 2023.
supportResources.description: ASH clinical guideline on megaloblastic anemia — covers B12 deficiency, differential diagnosis, and management protocols including inherited causes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pernicious Anemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause megaloblastic anemia with B12 deficiency, but pernicious anemia is caused by intrinsic factor deficiency (autoimmune destruction of parietal cells) and presents in adults (usually over age 40), not in young children. Imerslund-Grasbeck has childhood onset, may have proteinuria, and has an abnormal Schilling test stage II (pernicious anemia has a normal stage II because intrinsic factor corrects it). Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies are negative in Imerslund-Grasbeck.

confusedWithConditions.name: Folate Deficiency Anemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause macrocytic megaloblastic anemia, but folate deficiency has a normal serum B12, normal MMA (MMA is elevated only in B12 deficiency), and does not cause the neurological complications of B12 deficiency. Folate deficiency is typically dietary in origin, common in malnourished or alcoholic patients, and responds to oral folate replacement.

confusedWithConditions.name: Myelodysplastic Syndrome (MDS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MDS can cause macrocytic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils, but MDS is a clonal bone marrow disorder of older adults (usually over age 60), not children. Bone marrow in MDS shows dysplastic changes and cytogenetic abnormalities (5q deletion, monosomy 7). B12, MMA, and genetic testing for AMN/CUBN distinguish Imerslund-Grasbeck from MDS.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ALL in children causes anemia, but the anemia is typically normocytic or mildly macrocytic, with circulating blast cells on peripheral smear. ALL also causes thrombocytopenia and neutropenia from marrow infiltration, not the isolated megaloblastic anemia of Imerslund-Grasbeck. Bone marrow examination distinguishes ALL from B12 deficiency.

relatedConditions.name: Pernicious Anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both pernicious anemia and Imerslund-Grasbeck cause B12 deficiency via intestinal malabsorption, but they have different mechanisms — intrinsic factor deficiency vs. receptor dysfunction — and different clinical features (age of onset, proteinuria). Both are treated with B12 injections.

relatedConditions.name: Folate Deficiency Anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both cause megaloblastic anemia but B12 deficiency is the more urgent diagnosis because it causes irreversible neurological damage — all macrocytic anemias must have B12 and MMA checked before assuming folate deficiency is the cause.

relatedConditions.name: Drug-Induced Folate Deficiency Anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Drug-induced folate deficiency is a common acquired cause of megaloblastic anemia, distinct from the inherited Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome. Both can present with macrocytic anemia but have entirely different management approaches.

faqs.question: What is selective vitamin B12 malabsorption syndrome?
faqs.answer: Also called Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome, this is a rare genetic disorder where the intestine cannot absorb B12 because the cubilin-amionless receptor (needed to take up B12-intrinsic factor from food) is defective. This causes B12 deficiency with megaloblastic anemia, treated with regular B12 injections.

faqs.question: Is Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes — Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome is autosomal recessive. Both parents must carry a pathogenic AMN or CUBN gene variant for a child to be affected. Siblings of an affected child have a 25% chance of being affected. Carriers have no symptoms.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome and pernicious anemia?
faqs.answer: Both cause B12 deficiency and megaloblastic anemia, but pernicious anemia is an autoimmune disease where intrinsic factor is absent (in adults, usually over age 40), while Imerslund-Grasbeck is a genetic receptor defect that presents in early childhood. The key distinguishing feature is that Imerslund-Grasbeck patients often have proteinuria, which is absent in pernicious anemia.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include fatigue, pallor, glossitis (sore smooth tongue), failure to thrive in children, numbness and tingling in hands and feet, and gait disturbance. Left untreated, B12 deficiency causes irreversible neurological damage (subacute combined degeneration).

faqs.question: How is Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The diagnosis involves a CBC showing macrocytic anemia, low serum B12 with elevated MMA (confirming B12 deficiency), proteinuria (present in 50-70% of cases), and genetic testing for AMN and CUBN gene variants. Schilling test stage II is abnormal (unlike pernicious anemia).

faqs.question: What is the treatment for Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome?
faqs.answer: The treatment is regular intramuscular injections of hydroxocobalamin (B12) — typically 1 mg IM every 2-4 weeks for life. This bypasses the intestinal absorption defect. Oral B12 is NOT effective because it is also malabsorbed. Neurological symptoms improve within weeks to months of starting treatment.

faqs.question: Can Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome be cured?
faqs.answer: Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome is a genetic condition that cannot be cured. However, B12 injections provide complete hematological correction and prevent neurological complications for life. With lifelong treatment, patients have a normal life expectancy and quality of life.

faqs.question: Why does Imerslund-Grasbeck syndrome cause proteinuria?
faqs.answer: The cubilin-amionless receptor is also expressed in the kidneys, where it reabsorbs filtered proteins from the glomerular filtrate back into the blood. When this receptor is defective, proteins spill into the urine (proteinuria). This occurs in 50-70% of Imerslund-Grasbeck patients. The proteinuria does not typically progress to significant kidney disease.

faqs.question: What neurological damage does B12 deficiency cause?
faqs.answer: B12 deficiency causes subacute combined degeneration — demyelination of the spinal cord's dorsal columns (causing loss of vibration and position sense) and corticospinal tracts (causing weakness and spasticity). Symptoms include numbness, tingling, difficulty walking (especially in the dark), and eventually paralysis. This damage can be irreversible if treatment is delayed beyond 6-12 months.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from B12 deficiency in Imerslund-Grasbeck?
faqs.answer: Hematological response is rapid — reticulocytosis begins within 3-5 days, hemoglobin normalizes within 4-8 weeks. Neurological symptoms begin improving within 2-4 weeks and continue for up to 6 months. Complete neurological recovery is expected if treatment starts before irreversible damage occurs.

faqs.question: Is genetic testing available for Imerslund-Grasbeck?
faqs.answer: Yes — genetic testing for pathogenic variants in AMN and CUBN genes is available at clinical genetics laboratories. It confirms the diagnosis, distinguishes it from pernicious anemia, and enables family carrier testing and prenatal diagnosis for future pregnancies.

faqs.question: What is methylmalonic acid (MMA) and why is it measured?
faqs.answer: MMA is a compound that accumulates in the blood when B12 is deficient because B12 is needed for the enzyme that converts MMA to succinyl-CoA. MMA is elevated in B12 deficiency but normal in folate deficiency — this distinguishes B12 from folate deficiency as the cause of megaloblastic anemia. Serum MMA is a more sensitive marker of cellular B12 deficiency than serum B12 itself.
